publications.ref: Geyer PE, Wewer Albrechtsen NJ, Tyanova S, Grassl N, Iepsen EW, Lundgren J, Madsbad S, Holst JJ, Torekov SS, Mann M. Proteomics reveals the effects of sustained weight loss on the human plasma proteome. Mol Syst Biol. 2016 Dec 22;12(12):901

Characteristics[OrganismPart].evidence: plasma proteomes, mass spectrometry‐based plasma proteome profiling, plasma proteome

Characteristics[MaterialType].evidence: plasma proteomes of a cohort, plasma proteome profiling, measured 1,294 plasma proteomes

Characteristics[Label].evidence: mass spectrometry‐based plasma proteome profiling with no mention of chemical labeling methods

Comment[AcquisitionMethod].evidence: mass spectrometry‐based plasma proteome profiling, measured 1,294 plasma proteomes

Characteristics[Disease].reasoning: Study focuses on obese individuals and mentions metabolic conditions and insulin resistance
Characteristics[Disease].needs: Confirmation if obesity is considered the primary disease state or if participants had additional metabolic diseases

Characteristics[Treatment].reasoning: Study involves sustained weight loss intervention with specific timeline mentioned
Characteristics[Treatment].needs: Details about the specific weight loss protocol or method used

STEP1_OVERVIEW.summary: This is a human plasma proteomics study using label-free shotgun proteomics on 43 obese individuals undergoing weight loss intervention. The study involves longitudinal sampling (baseline, after 8 weeks weight loss, after 1 year maintenance) with 1,294 total plasma proteome measurements. Key unresolved questions include specific treatment protocols and whether obesity should be classified as the primary disease characteristic.

Characteristics[CleavageAgent].evidence: Trypsin and LysC (1:100 μg of enzyme to micrograms of protein ratio) were added to the mixture

Characteristics[AlkylationReagent].evidence: cysteine carbamidomethylation as a fixed modification

Characteristics[ReductionReagent].evidence: Reduction and alkylation were carried out at 95°C for 10 min

Characteristics[Modification].evidence: N‐terminal acetylation and methionine oxidations as variable modifications

Characteristics[Modification].1.evidence: N‐terminal acetylation and methionine oxidations as variable modifications

Characteristics[Modification].2.evidence: cysteine carbamidomethylation as a fixed modification

Characteristics[Depletion].evidence: depletion of the top 20 most abundant plasma proteins by a combination of two immunodepletion kits

Comment[Separation].evidence: Purified peptides were separated on 40‐cm HPLC columns with ReproSil‐Pur C18‐AQ 1.9 μm resin

STEP2_SAMPLE_PREP.summary: Sample preparation details are fully characterized: trypsin/LysC digestion, TCEP reduction, CAA alkylation, and label-free quantitation. Key modifications include N-terminal acetylation, methionine oxidation (variable), and cysteine carbamidomethylation (fixed). Abundant protein depletion using immunodepletion kits was performed for library building. LC separation used 40-cm C18 columns. No fractionation, enrichment, or synthetic compounds were used.

Comment[Instrument].evidence: LC‐MS instrumentation consisting of an EASY‐nLC 1000 ultra‐high‐pressure system (Thermo Fisher Scientific), which was combined with a Q Exactive HF Orbitrap (Thermo Fisher Scientific)

Comment[FragmentationMethod].evidence: Fragmentation of precursor ions was performed by higher‐energy C‐trap dissociation (HCD) with a normalized collision energy of 27 eV

Comment[AcquisitionMethod].evidence: MS data were acquired with a Top15 data‐dependent MS/MS scan method (topN method)

Comment[IonizationType].evidence: combined with a Q Exactive HF Orbitrap (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and a nano‐electrospray ion source (Thermo Fisher Scientific)

Comment[MS2MassAnalyzer].evidence: Q Exactive HF Orbitrap (Thermo Fisher Scientific)

Comment[CollisionEnergy].evidence: higher‐energy C‐trap dissociation (HCD) with a normalized collision energy of 27 eV

Comment[PrecursorMassTolerance].evidence: Peptide identification was performed with an initial precursor mass deviation up to 7 ppm

Comment[NumberOfMissedCleavages].evidence: a maximum of two missed cleavages were allowed

Comment[GradientTime].evidence: around 1 μg peptides was used for 45‐min runs and for each fraction of the deep plasma dataset

Comment[FlowRateChromatogram].evidence: followed by a 3.5‐min wash of 98% buffer B at a flow rate of 350 nl/min

STEP3_MS_SETTINGS.summary: Mass spectrometry instrumentation and parameters are fully characterized. The study used Q Exactive HF Orbitrap with nano-ESI, HCD fragmentation at 27 eV, and Top15 DDA acquisition. Search parameters included 7 ppm precursor tolerance, 20 ppm fragment tolerance, maximum 2 missed cleavages. LC gradient was 45 minutes at 350 nl/min flow rate. All key MS parameters have been definitively identified from the methods section.

Comment[FractionIdentifier].evidence: No fractionation was performed based on methods section stating label-free quantitation without mention of fractionation

Characteristics[NumberOfBiologicalReplic.evidence: 43 obese individuals undergoing weight loss intervention

Characteristics[NumberOfSamples].evidence: 1,294 total plasma proteome measurements with sampling at 7 time points (weeks −8, 0, 4, 13, 26, 39, 52)

FactorValue[Treatment].evidence: Study participants followed a weekly supervised very low‐calorie powder diet (800 kcal per day) for 8 weeks followed by weight maintenance phase

FactorValue[Disease].evidence: 58 obese study participants were recruited with BMI between 30 and 40 kg/m2

Characteristics[BiologicalReplicate].reasoning: Raw file names contain participant identifiers (e.g., 24_1, 40_4) but exact mapping to biological replicates unclear
Characteristics[BiologicalReplicate].needs: Metadata file mapping raw file names to participant IDs

Characteristics[NumberOfTechnicalReplica.reasoning: Most samples appear to be single measurements, but depleted reference samples were measured in triplicate
Characteristics[NumberOfTechnicalReplica.needs: Clarification if study samples had technical replicates beyond the reference library

FactorValue[Compound].reasoning: Half participants received 1.2 mg liraglutide daily, and Cambridge Weight Plan products were used during intervention
FactorValue[Compound].needs: Per-sample metadata indicating which participants received liraglutide

STEP4_RAW_FILES.summary: Raw file analysis confirms 43 obese participants with 1,294 total measurements across 7 time points during weight loss intervention. Treatment factors include 8-week very low-calorie diet followed by maintenance phase, with half receiving liraglutide. File naming contains participant and time point information but exact biological replicate mapping requires additional metadata. No fractionation was performed.
